name: Moscovium
name_origin: Named after Moscow Oblast, the Russian region where the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (JINR) Dubna laboratory is located.

discovery.discoverer: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (JINR) Dubna team led by Yuri Oganessian, in collaboration with Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory

occurrence.abundance_summary: Moscovium does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators via the hot-fusion reaction 48Ca + 243Am.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

reactivity_summary: No chemistry of moscovium has been experimentally characterized. By analogy with its lighter group 15 homologs (antimony, bismuth) it is predicted to be a post-transition metal favoring the +1 oxidation state due to the relativistically stabilized 7s2 pair, with the +3 state likely accessible but less stable than for bismuth.
isotopes_summary: All moscovium isotopes are radioactive. The longest-lived confirmed isotope is 290Mc with a half-life on the order of a fraction of a second to a second; alpha-decay chains from moscovium have been used to confirm nihonium and lower-Z superheavy isotopes.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Moscovium
